title: FTC proposes AI-accuracy policy that argues state AI laws may be federally preempted
subtitle: The Commission's Section 5 statement names Colorado's AI Act as a preemption target, calls output-altering state rules potentially deceptive, and opens a public comment window that closes July 31.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: The Federal Trade Commission on July 7 published a proposed policy statement in the Federal Register arguing that AI companies that distort their models' outputs to achieve undisclosed objectives — including compliance with state-level AI laws — may be engaging in deceptive practices under Section 5 of the FTC Act. The statement was issued pursuant to Executive Order 14365, signed by President Trump in December 2025, which directed the FTC to clarify how Section 5 applies to AI models and to address how state laws requiring alterations to model outputs can conflict with federal law. Colorado's Artificial Intelligence Act is named as an example of a state law the FTC believes is impliedly preempted to the extent it conflicts with the federal regulatory scheme. The public comment period closes July 31.

The mechanism is subtle and worth reading carefully. The FTC is not claiming direct authority to strike down state AI laws. It is arguing that when a company alters its model outputs to comply with a state disparate-impact statute — Colorado's law is the archetype — the resulting distortion of outputs is itself a deceptive act under federal consumer-protection law, and that federal law preempts the state requirement to the extent the two conflict. This is the same doctrinal move federal regulators used against state consumer-finance rules in the 2010s. It is a very effective move because it does not require Congress to pass anything.

The timing is not incidental. Illinois governor JB Pritzker signed the nation-leading Artificial Intelligence Safety Measures Act one day earlier on July 6. California's SB 53 is on the books. New York's frontier legislation cleared crossover in May. Colorado's law is already in force after a June 30 replacement package. Every serious US state-level AI regulation was drafted assuming states retained substantive authority. The FTC statement is a shot across the bow of that assumption, and it lands during a comment window short enough to force the states, industry, and civil society groups to engage before the July 31 deadline. Watch which state attorneys general file comments — the pattern will tell you which states are prepared to litigate.

A takeaway for learners: US AI law right now has two competing centers of gravity — federal preemption pushed by the executive branch, and state-level experimentation from Sacramento, Springfield, Albany, and Denver. Neither has settled. If you are working with AI in a US company today, understand that your compliance obligations will change materially depending on how this fight resolves, and probably in the next six months. Read the Federal Register comment — the underlying document at matter number P264200 is short — before you form a view. Policy that will shape what AI products can legally do in 2027 is being written right now, and the comment window is your one chance to be part of it.

sources.title: FTC Seeks Public Comment on Policy Statement Addressing AI Accuracy — Federal Trade Commission
